content.body: Organic chemistry is the study of carbon-based compounds. Carbon is unique — it can form 4 bonds and chain with other carbon atoms to create millions of different molecules. Most compounds in living things and fuels are organic.

Hydrocarbons contain only Carbon and Hydrogen.

ALKANES (saturated — only single bonds):
General formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂
Methane: CH₄, Ethane: C₂H₆, Propane: C₃H₈, Butane: C₄H₁₀

ALKENES (unsaturated — contain a C=C double bond):
General formula: CₙH₂ₙ
Ethene: C₂H₄, Propene: C₃H₆, Butene: C₄H₈

content.title: Properties of Alkanes
content.body:
• Good fuels (combust in oxygen releasing CO₂, H₂O and lots of energy)
• Less reactive than alkenes
• Complete combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O
• Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): produces CO (poisonous) and soot (carbon)

Crude oil is a mixture of alkanes separated by fractional distillation.

content.title: Properties and Reactions of Alkenes
content.body:
Alkenes are more reactive than alkanes due to the C=C double bond.

Addition reactions: the double bond opens and atoms add across it.
Hydration: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (ethanol — making alcohol!)
Hydrogenation: C₂H₄ + H₂ → C₂H₆ (making margarine from vegetable oil)
Halogenation: C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (decolourises bromine water — TEST for alkene!)

Test for alkenes: bromine water (orange/yellow solution).
• Alkene: bromine decolourises (turns colourless) — double bond reacts with Br₂
• Alkane: no colour change

Polymerisation: many small alkene molecules (monomers) join to form a long-chain polymer.
Ethene → poly(ethene) = polythene/polyethylene. Used in plastic bags, bottles.

questions.question: What is the general formula for alkanes?
questions.explanation: Alkanes follow CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. E.g. n=1: CH₄ (methane), n=2: C₂H₆ (ethane).

questions.question: What distinguishes alkenes from alkanes?
options: Alkenes have a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C)
questions.answer: Alkenes have a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C)
questions.explanation: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one C=C double bond.

questions.question: The molecular formula of propane is:
questions.explanation: Propane: n=3 in CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ = C₃H₈.

questions.question: How do you test for an alkene?
questions.explanation: Alkenes decolourise orange/yellow bromine water because the C=C double bond reacts with Br₂ in an addition reaction.

questions.question: Complete combustion of methane produces:
questions.explanation: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Complete combustion (plenty of oxygen) always gives CO₂ and H₂O.

questions.question: Incomplete combustion produces the dangerous gas:
questions.explanation: Incomplete combustion (limited oxygen) produces carbon monoxide (CO) — a colourless, odourless, toxic gas.

questions.question: Ethene + Br₂ → ?
questions.explanation: Addition reaction: C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (1,2-dibromoethane). The double bond opens and bromine adds across it.

questions.question: Polymerisation of ethene produces:
questions.explanation: Many ethene (C₂H₄) monomers join in addition polymerisation to form poly(ethene) = polythene.

questions.question: Ethene + H₂O → (fermentation catalyst, high pressure)
questions.explanation: Hydration of ethene: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (ethanol). This is the industrial method of making alcohol.

questions.question: Crude oil is separated into fractions by:
questions.explanation: Crude oil (a mixture of hydrocarbons with different boiling points) is separated by fractional distillation in a fractionating column.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: The rate of reaction is how fast a chemical reaction occurs — how quickly reactants are converted to products. Understanding and controlling reaction rates is crucial in industrial chemistry, medicine, and food preservation.

Rate of reaction = change in amount / time

Measured by:
• Rate of disappearance of a reactant
• Rate of appearance of a product
• Change in mass, volume of gas, colour, or turbidity over time

content.title: Factors Affecting Rate of Reaction
content.body:
1. CONCENTRATION: More reactant particles → more frequent collisions → faster rate

2. TEMPERATURE: Higher temperature → particles have more kinetic energy → move faster → more frequent AND more energetic collisions

3. SURFACE AREA: Smaller particle size → greater surface area → more particles exposed → faster rate. Powders react faster than lumps.

4. CATALYST: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up. Lowers activation energy. Enzymes are biological catalysts.

5. PRESSURE (gases only): Higher pressure → molecules closer together → more collisions → faster rate

content.title: Collision Theory
content.body:
For a reaction to occur, particles must:
1. Collide with each other
2. Have sufficient energy (≥ activation energy)

Activation Energy: the minimum energy needed for a collision to result in a reaction.

A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with LOWER activation energy → more collisions are successful → faster reaction.

Practical example — marble chips + hydrochloric acid:
CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂
Rate can be measured by: volume of CO₂ produced per minute, or decrease in mass of reaction flask.
Using powder (more surface area) or concentrated acid (higher concentration) increases the rate.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT increase the rate of a reaction?
questions.explanation: Decreasing concentration means fewer particles in a given volume → fewer collisions → slower rate.

questions.question: Why does powdering a solid increase the reaction rate?
options: Powders have greater surface area for collisions
questions.answer: Powders have greater surface area for collisions
questions.explanation: Smaller particles expose more surface area for reactant particles to collide with.

questions.question: A catalyst:
options: Is used up in the reaction
Speeds up reaction without being consumed
questions.answer: Speeds up reaction without being consumed
questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds up a reaction and can be recovered unchanged at the end.

questions.question: Activation energy is:
options: Minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur
questions.answer: Minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur
questions.explanation: Activation energy is the energy barrier — collisions must have at least this much energy to result in a reaction.

questions.question: How does increasing temperature increase reaction rate?
options: Particles have more energy → faster and more frequent collisions
questions.answer: Particles have more energy → faster and more frequent collisions
questions.explanation: At higher temperatures, particles move faster (more kinetic energy), collide more often and with greater force.

questions.question: Enzymes are:
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts produced by living organisms that speed up biochemical reactions.

questions.question: Which graph shape shows a typical reaction rate over time?
options: Starts fast then levels off as reactants are used up
questions.answer: Starts fast then levels off as reactants are used up
questions.explanation: As reactants are used up, concentration decreases → fewer collisions → rate decreases → graph levels off when reaction stops.

questions.question: Marble chips react with HCl. Using higher concentration HCl will:
options: Increase the rate of CO₂ production
questions.answer: Increase the rate of CO₂ production
questions.explanation: Higher HCl concentration means more H⁺ ions → more frequent collisions with marble → faster rate.

questions.question: A catalyst works by:
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy, allowing more collisions to be successful.

questions.question: How can you measure the rate of reaction CaCO₃ + HCl → CO₂?
options: Measure decrease in mass of flask
questions.answer: Measure decrease in mass of flask
questions.explanation: CO₂ gas escapes the flask, causing the mass to decrease. Rate = decrease in mass per unit time.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons with a C=C double bond. Alcohols contain -OH group. Carboxylic acids contain -COOH group. These are three important homologous series.

Alkenes:
CₙH₂ₙ (general formula)
Double bond reacts by ADDITION (saturation)
Test: decolourise bromine water (Br₂/water)→ from orange to colourless
Polymerisation: nCH₂=CH₂ → (−CH₂−CH₂−)ₙ (poly(ethene))

content.title: Alcohols
content.body:
CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH (general formula)
Methanol CH₃OH, Ethanol C₂H₅OH
Production: fermentation (glucose + yeast → ethanol + CO₂)
Reactions: combustion, oxidation to carboxylic acid, esterification
Ethanol: drinks, fuel, solvent

content.title: Carboxylic Acids
content.body:
CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH
Methanoic acid HCOOH, Ethanoic acid CH₃COOH (vinegar)
Properties: weak acids, lower pH than strong acids, sour taste
Reactions: with alkalis, carbonates, alcohols (esterification)
Esters: fragrant compounds used in perfumes, flavourings

questions.question: Alkenes are characterised by...
questions.explanation: Alkenes have at least one C=C double bond — they are unsaturated (can add more atoms across the double bond).

questions.question: The test for an alkene uses...
options: Bromine water (decolourised from orange to colourless)
questions.answer: Bromine water (decolourised from orange to colourless)
questions.explanation: Alkenes add Br₂ across the double bond. Orange bromine water turns colourless — positive test for C=C.

questions.question: Ethanol is produced industrially by...
questions.explanation: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ using yeast enzymes (anaerobic). Used for biofuels and drinks industry.

questions.question: General formula of alcohols is...
questions.explanation: Alcohols: CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH. The hydroxyl (-OH) functional group defines this homologous series.

questions.question: Esterification is a reaction between...
questions.explanation: Esterification: alcohol + carboxylic acid ⇌ ester + water. Produces sweet-smelling esters used in flavourings.

questions.question: Carboxylic acids are described as weak because...
options: They are not fully ionised in solution
questions.answer: They are not fully ionised in solution
questions.explanation: Weak acids (like ethanoic acid) only partially dissociate in water → lower [H⁺] than strong acid of same concentration.

questions.question: Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) reacts with sodium carbonate producing...
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂↑. Test for carboxylic acids: add Na₂CO₃, watch for bubbles.

questions.question: Addition polymerisation requires monomers with...
questions.explanation: Addition polymerisation uses alkene monomers. The double bond opens and monomers join repeatedly.

questions.question: Biofuels from fermentation are renewable because...
options: The crop plants are continuously regrown absorbing CO₂
questions.answer: The crop plants are continuously regrown absorbing CO₂
questions.explanation: Biofuel fermentation releases CO₂, but growing new crops absorbs CO₂. Net CO₂ contribution is approximately zero — carbon neutral.

questions.question: Oxidation of ethanol (using acidified K₂Cr₂O₇) gives...
questions.explanation: Ethanol + strong oxidising agent → ethanoic acid (via ethanal). Dichromate changes from orange to green as it oxidises the alcohol.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

lessons.title: Alkanes and Alkenes — Properties and Reactions

content.body:
Alkanes CₙH₂ₙ₊₂: saturated (all single bonds)
Methane CH₄, Ethane C₂H₆, Propane C₃H₈, Butane C₄H₁₀
Reactions: combustion, substitution (with halogens/UV)

Alkenes CₙH₂ₙ: unsaturated (C=C double bond)
Ethene C₂H₄, Propene C₃H₆
Reactions: addition reactions (H₂, Br₂, HX, H₂O)
Bromine water test: orange→colourless

Polymerisation: many monomers → polymer
n(CH₂=CH₂) → (-CH₂-CH₂-)ₙ (poly(ethene))

content.title: Addition Reactions
content.body:
Ethene + Br₂ → 1,2-dibromoethane (decolourises bromine water)
Ethene + H₂ → ethane (Ni catalyst, 150°C)
Ethene + H₂O → ethanol (H₃PO₄ catalyst, steam)
Ethene + HBr → bromoethane (Markovnikov: H adds to C with more H)

content.title: Cracking
content.body:
Long chain alkanes → shorter useful chains
Thermal cracking: high temperature, high pressure
Catalytic cracking: zeolite catalyst, 450°C
Produces alkenes (valuable for polymerisation and chemical industry)
C₁₀H₂₂ → C₅H₁₂ + C₅H₁₀ (pentane + pentene)

questions.question: General formula of alkanes
questions.explanation: Alkanes: saturated hydrocarbons. CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ (methane CH₄, n=1: 2+2=4 H)

questions.question: Alkenes are unsaturated because
options: they contain a C=C double bond
questions.answer: they contain a C=C double bond
questions.explanation: Unsaturated: contains C=C (or C≡C) — can react by addition across the double bond

questions.question: Test for alkene: bromine water changes
questions.explanation: Alkene + Br₂(aq) → dibromoalkane (colourless). Orange bromine water decolourises instantly

questions.question: Ethene + H₂O (steam, catalyst) produces
questions.explanation: Hydration of ethene: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (industrial ethanol production)

questions.question: Cracking of long chain alkanes produces
questions.explanation: Cracking breaks large molecules into smaller alkanes and alkenes (unsaturated)

questions.question: Addition polymerisation of ethene produces
questions.explanation: n(CH₂=CH₂) → (-CH₂CH₂-)ₙ polyethylene/poly(ethene)

questions.question: Substitution of methane with chlorine (UV light):
questions.explanation: Free radical substitution (UV): one H replaced by Cl, HCl produced

questions.question: Incomplete combustion of alkanes produces
questions.explanation: Limited oxygen: C not fully oxidised → CO (toxic) and unburned carbon (soot)

questions.question: Markovnikov's rule: HBr adds to propene CH₃CH=CH₂ giving mainly
questions.explanation: H adds to CH end (more H), Br to middle C (Markovnikov). Product: 2-bromopropane

questions.question: Catalyst for industrial ethene hydration to ethanol
questions.explanation: Steam + ethene over H₃PO₄ (or H₂SO₄) catalyst at 300°C, 70atm

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Organic Chemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Organic Chemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Organic Chemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Organic Chemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Organic Chemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Organic Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Organic Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Organic Chemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Organic Chemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Organic Chemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Organic Chemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

Common examination questions on Organic Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Organic Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
